coredata.dc:description: Single atom nanozyme (SAzyme) based on carbon dots (CDs) has showed great potential in oncotherapy via ultrasmall size-reinforced atomically dispersed catalytic sites. However, its curative effect is still unsatisfactory due to complex tumor microenvironment and intrinsic resistance. Herein, a coordinated carbon dots (CCDs)-integrated ZIF-8 nanoassembly (Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF) was constructed by loading paclitaxel and coating with rutin for synergistic catalytic/chemotherapy. Benefiting from inherited metal-polyphenol coordination, the CCDs exhibited superior peroxidase-like (POD) activity and served as a SAzyme to produce large amounts of hydroxyl radical, resulting in radical-dependent cell cycle arrest. With the assistance of GLUT receptor-mediated endocytosis, the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF triggered the tumor-targeted killing and migration inhibition to suppress tumor progression. This study highlights a promising avenue to broaden the design and applications of CDs-based SAzyme in tumor treatment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. (A) TEM images of ZIF-8, (B) CCDs, and (C) Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. (D) Size distribution of ZIF-8, PTX@ZIF, CCDs-PTX@ZIF, and Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. (E) Surface zeta potential, (F) XRD spectrum, (G) FTIR spectra of ZIF-8 and Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. (H) XPS spectra of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF, (I) High-resolution XPS spectra of Fe2p of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. (J) Corresponding element mapping images of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF.

Validation of free radical and ROS production in vitro. (A) Schematic illustration of the possible catalytic mechanism of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF with TMB or MV as substrates. (B, C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of TMB and MV solutions (pH 6.5) under different treatments. Inset: Photos of the corresponding solutions. (D) Cellular viability of 4T1 cell after incubation with CCDs NPs in the presence of H2O2. (E) Fluorescencent images of intracellular ROS in 4T1 cells. Scale bar: 100 μm.

Biocompatibility and in vitro tumor cell killing ability of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. (A) Hemolysis rate and corresponding digital photos. (B) Relative cell viability of ZIF-8 NPs co-cultured with MEF cells and 4T1 cells for 48 h. (C) Relative cell viability of 4T1 cells co-cultured with different concentrations of PTX@ZIF and Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF for 24 h. (D) Fluorescence images of FDA/PI stained 4T1 cells. Scale bar: 100 μm.

In vitro antitumor mechanism analysis of the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. (A) Flow cytometry analysis of 4T1 cells apoptosis induced by different treatments with Annexin-V-FITC/PI staining. (G1: Control, G2: ZIF-8, G3: PTX@ZIF, G4: Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF). (B) Prussian blue stained MEF and 4T1 cells were obtained after incubation with Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF for 4 h (Prussian blue is used to mark intracellular iron). Scale bar: 100 μm. (C) Fluorescence images of 4T1 cells incubated with RhoB-labeled Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF for 2 h, treated or untreated with free rutin. Scale bar: 100 μm. (D) In vitro wound scratch assay with different treatments and (E) corresponding quantitative analysis of cell migration rates. Scale bar: 100 μm.

In vitro Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF inhibitory ability of tumor cells. (A) Migration and (C) invasion of 4T1 cells after incubation with various treatments using transwell assay, as well as co-responding number of migrating (B) and invading cells (D). Scale bar: 500 μm. (E) Colony formation images of 4T1 cells after incubation with various treatments and (F) corresponding quantitive analysis of clone number. (G1: Control, G2: ZIF-8, G3: PTX@ZIF, G4: Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF).

Characterization of MR imaging performance of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. (A) T 1-weighted images of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF at different concentrations (0, 100, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 2000 μg/mL). (B) Linear relationship between Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF concentration and T 1-weighted longitudinal relaxation signal intensity. (C) Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF T 1-weighted cross-sectional and (D) sagittal imaging of 4T1 tumor-bearing mice before and after injection. (Red circle represents tumor region).

Evaluation of antitumor effect in vivo of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. (A) Digital photographs of the tumor area on days 0 and on days 20. (B) Tumor volume curves of each mouse after various treatments. (C) The mean tumor volume curve within 20 days, (D) Average body weight growth curves and (E) survival curves of mice after various treatments. (n = 5). (G1: Control, G2: ZIF-8, G3: PTX@ZIF, G4:CCDs-ZIF, G5: Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF).
Schematic diagram of preparation process and synergistic catalytic/chemo-therapy of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF.

head.ce:title: Fe-coordinated carbon dots with single atom nanozyme catalytic activity for synergistic catalytic/chemo-therapy in breast cancer

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Single atom nanozyme (SAzyme) based on carbon dots (CDs) has showed great potential in oncotherapy via ultrasmall size-reinforced atomically dispersed catalytic sites. However, its curative effect is still unsatisfactory due to complex tumor microenvironment and intrinsic resistance. Herein, a coordinated carbon dots (CCDs)-integrated ZIF-8 nanoassembly (Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF) was constructed by loading paclitaxel and coating with rutin for synergistic catalytic/chemotherapy. Benefiting from inherited metal-polyphenol coordination, the CCDs exhibited superior peroxidase-like (POD) activity and served as a SAzyme to produce large amounts of hydroxyl radical, resulting in radical-dependent cell cycle arrest. With the assistance of GLUT receptor-mediated endocytosis, the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF triggered the tumor-targeted killing and migration inhibition to suppress tumor progression. This study highlights a promising avenue to broaden the design and applications of CDs-based SAzyme in tumor treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer is one of the three most common cancers globally. In recent years, its incidence has risen to the top among all cancers, becoming the leading cause of cancer-related deaths in women worldwide [1]. The uncontrolled proliferation of malignant cells results in abnormal growth and invasion of breast tissue, posing a significant threat to women's health [2]. While traditional surgical methods have seen advancements, they still cause irreversible damage to normal tissues. To advance cancer treatment, it is crucial to explore non-surgical options [3]. An emerging method for treating malignant tumors, known as Chemodynamic Therapy (CDT), involves the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) that induce oxidative stress and cell death [4–6]. This process primarily utilizes Fenton or Fenton-like reactions to produce an excess of free radicals, resulting in permanent oxidative damage to macromolecules [7]. CDT is considered a cancer treatment strategy that employs nanomedicines containing transition metals to react with endogenous hydrogen peroxide, generating ROS [8]. Due to their potent oxidizing capabilities, ROS can completely disrupt the structure and function of cancer cells. Increasing evidences suggest that metal-polyphenol nanocomposites, composed of plant polyphenols and metal ion complexes, hold great promise for the treatment of malignant tumors due to their low side effects and synergistic therapeutic combinations [9–11]. Nanomaterials possess numerous unique physical and chemical properties, offering immense potential in the biomedical field and providing a platform for the development of novel precision diagnostics and therapeutic drugs [12–15].

Single-atom nanozymes (SAzyme) are a class of emerging catalytic nanomaterials which are characterized by their atomically dispersed catalytic sites and precisely coordinated electronic structures [16–18]. With these inherent advantages, SAzyme-based catalytic therapy have attracted extensive attention in the field of tumor treatments [19]. The catalytic performances of SAzyme can be tailored via the regulation of the interactions between the metal active centers and supporting matrices at atomic levels [20]. Given their well-defined phase structures and ultrasmall size, carbon dots (CDs) can be served as an ideal supporting matrice to form the CDs-supported Fe single-atom nanozymes (CDs-SAzyme) by integrating active metal atoms, resulting in extraordinary catalytic activity [21]. For instance, Han et al. reported a CDs-supported Fe SAzyme with high content of pyrrolic N and ultrasmall size (ph-CDs-Fe SAzyme) by a phenanthroline-mediated ligand-assisted strategy [22]. Qin et al. provided a CD-supported Fe single-atom nanocatalysts in porous silica through in situ high-temperature carbonization strategy [23]. However, tumor complex microenvironment and intrisic resistance severely compromise the antitumor effect of CDs-SAzyme [24–26].

Recently, nanotechnology has experienced rapid growth, especially with the advent of new nanomaterials, which have provided innovative approaches and methods for treating many major diseases. For instance, Metal-Organic Framework materials (MOFs) as drug nanocarriers have garnered widespread attention in oncology due to their unique properties. [27–32]. MOF, a porous material formed by the coordination of organic framework and metal ions, has a large specific surface area and high adsorption performance, making it ideal for delivering poorly soluble anti-cancer drugs [33–37]. Among MOFs, zeolite imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) stands out as an ideal drug carrier with exceptional stability and encapsulation efficiency for chemotherapy drugs [38–42]. Notably, ZIF-8 exhibits non-toxicity, good biocompatibility, and pH-responsive biodegradation properties, which enables it stable in the bloodstream and prevents premature drug release that could lead to adverse reactions [43–45].

Based on the above findings, we designed a coordinated carbon dots-integrated ZIF-8 nanoassembly by loading paclitaxel (PTX) and surface adsorbing rutin (Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF) for synergistic catalytic/chemo-therapy [46]. Recent studies have shown that plant polyphenol was capable of converting Fe3+ into Fe2+ under acidic conditions, then resulting in irreversible oxidative damage to tumor cells via the Fenton reaction [47–49]. In this work, we speculated that the CCDs derived from tannic acid (a typical plant polyphenol) might inherit this catalytic activity [50]. Moreover, the CCDs had ultra-small size and the ability to chelate metal ions, which endowed them with remarkable SAzyme-like catalytic activity. In addition, high level of glucose metabolism is considered as the notable characteristics of malignant tumor, thereby making it a potential tumor therapeutic targeting [51–55]. Based on this fact, rutin as a flavonoid containing glucose residues has been demonstrated to bind specifically glucose transporters on the surface of tumor cells [56]. Therefore, we chose rutin as targeting molecule to promote the accumulation of nanoparticels in tumor cells through GLUT-mediated endocytosis. This study offers a versatile nanoplatform via CDs-based SAzyme against tumor intrisic resistance (Scheme 1 ).

Paclitaxel (PTX) was purchased from Solarbio. Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2∙6H2O), 2-methylimidazole (2-MIM), tannic acid (TA), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), rutin, ferric chloride hexahydrate were purchased from Aladdin. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) K30 was purchased from Aladdin. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) was obtained from DaLian Meilum BiotechnoLogy Co., Ltd. RPMI-1640 medium, Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Hyclone (Logan, UT, USA). 2′-7′-Dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) was brought from GL Biochem Ltd. Cell counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was acquired from Vazyme. 4′,6-Diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) was gotten from Thermo Fisher Scientific.

To prepare the bare CDs, 0.85 g of TA was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water (ddH2O), then heated in a microwave oven for 10 min. After cooling to room temperature, the black precipitate was dissolved in 30 mL deionized water, then centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles. The supernatant was collected for dialysis and freeze-dried to obtain brown powder (CDs). To prepare the CCDs, 20 mg of CDs lyophilized powder was dissolved in 20 mL deionized water, and 100 μL of FeCl3 (10 mg/mL) was added into CDs solution under stirring for 30 min. Finally, the mixed solution was centrifugated at 3000 rpm for 15 min and then the supernatant was taken for dialysis and lyophilization to obtain CCDs powder.

148 mg of Zn(NO3)2∙6H2O and 10 mg of PTX was dissolved in 5 mL of DMSO and stirred for 10 min. Then, 4 mL of 2-MIM (82 mg/mL) in methanol solution was slowly added drop wise to the above solution and kept reacting for 10 min under constant stirring. The reaction solution was then centrifuged at 11000 rpm for 25 min, and the precipitate was obtained and washed three times using methanol to obtain PTX@ZIF.

Initially, 3 g of PVP was dissolved in 60 mL of ddH2O and stirred until thoroughly dissolved. 20 mg of PTX@ZIF was added into the PVP solution and incubated for 20 min. 1 mL of CCDs (4 mg/mL) aqueous solution was mixed with the PTX@ZIF solution and incubated for 1 h. Then, 0.1 mL of rutin (10 mg/mL) solution was added into the mixed solution and kept reacting for 1 h. The mixture was centrifuged at 11000 rpm for 30 min and the precipitate was collected after three times washing with ddH2O. Finally, the precipitate was lyophilized to obtain Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF powder.

The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of the ZIF-8 NPs, PTX@ZIF, CCDs-PTX@ZIF, and Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF were measured using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano series (Nano ZS90). The morphological images of these nanoparticles were obtained through Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20, USA). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was performed on a Nicolet iS20 spectrometer from Thermo Fisher Scientific. X-ray diffraction analysis was conducted using a Rigaku Ultra4 (Rigaku, Japan), with a scanning angle range of 5–50° and a scanning speed of 5° per minute. The valence state information of iron in Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF was recorded using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS).

All animal experiments were performed in accordance with the ethical guidelines of the Helsinki Declaration and was approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of the Jiangsu University. After anesthesia, the eyeball blood were obtained from BALB/c mice. Then, the blood was centrifuged and washed three times to get the red blood cells. Subsequently, 100 μL of the red blood cell suspension was mixed with 900 μL of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF solution at different concentrations (20, 50, 100, 200, 400 and 600 μg/mL), as well as with PBS (as a negative control group) or water (as a positive control group). After sitting at room temperature for 2 h, the mixed solutions were centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 1 min to collect the supernatant. Finally, the absorbance of the supernatant at 541 nm was measured under spectrophotometer.

The production of ·OH was detected through MV and TMB as a indicator, respectively. The methyl violet solution can be decolorized, and the TMB solution can be colored due to ·OH-mediated oxidation. Therefore, we chose MV and TMB as indicators to characterize the ability of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF to produce the ·OH under the presence of H2O2 via the Fenton reaction. The solutions listed below were prepared in advance: ferrous sulfate solution (0.2 mg/mL), Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF solution (0.5 mg/mL), TMB/MV solution (0.1 mg/mL, 0.1 mg/mL), and H2O2 solution (3 %). After reaction for 20 min, the absorbance of the combined solution was measured at 652 nm and 582 nm.

4T1 cells were seeded in a 24-well plate (2 × 104 cells each well) and cultured overnight. Subsequently, the 4T1 cells were co-incubated with ZIF-8 NPs, PTX@ZIF, CCDs-PTX@ZIF, and Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF (0.5 mg/mL) plus H2O2 (50 μM), respectively. After co-incubation for 2 h, the original medium was replaced with new serum-free culture medium containing DCFH-DA solution, and further incubated for 20 min. Finally, the cells were washed three times with PBS and observed under the fluorescence microscope.

CCK-8 assay kit was used to evaluate the cytocompatibility of ZIF-8 NPs on MEF cells (mouse embryonic fibroblast cell line) and 4T1 cells (mouse breast cancer cell line). These two cell lines were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well and cultured in an incubator at 37 °C with 5 % CO2, respectively. When the cell density reached 80 %, different concentrations of ZIF-8 NPs (0, 10, 20, 50, 100 and 200 μg/mL) were added into the each wells respectively. After 24 h of incubation, the cells were washed with PBS and then cultured with CCK-8 solution (10 %). After incubation for 1 h, the absorbance of the solution was measured at 450 nm under spectrophotometer.

4T1 cells were seeded into a 96-well plate with 1 × 104 cells each well and incubated overnight at 37 °C and 5 % CO2. Subsequently, the culture medium were replaced with fresh culture medium containing different concentrations of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF (0, 10, 20, 50, 100 and 200 μg/mL) respectively. After 24 h of incubation, the cells were washed with PBS and then re-incubated with CCK-8 (10 %) for 1 h, the absorbance of the solution was measured at 450 nm under spectrophotometer.

4T1 cells were seeded on a 24-well plate (2 × 104 cells each well), and allowed to culture overnight. Subsequently, the 4T1 cells were co-incubated with Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF, PTX@ZIF, and ZIF-8 NPs (0.5 mg/mL) respectively. After 24 h of incubation, the culture mediums were replaced with fresh culture medium containing FDA/PI dye solution for staining. After staining for 5 min, these cells were washed three times using PBS and observed under the fluorescence microscope.

4T1 cells were seeded on a 6-well plate (20 × 104 cells each well), and allowed to culture overnight. Subsequently, Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF, PTX@ZIF, PTX@ZIF, and ZIF-8 NPs were co-cultured with 4T1 cells respectively. After 24 h of incubation, the cells were collected. To prepare cell suspension, binding buffer was added following two PBS washes. The cells were then stained with Annexin V-FITC/PI dye solution for 15 min. Flow cytometry was employed as the analysis technique. Finally, the dispersion of the cell cycle was studied using FlowJo software.

4T1 and MEF cells (5 × 104 cells per well) were seeded in 6-well plates and cultured overnight. Subsequently, Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF nanoparticles were incubated with both 4T1 and MEF cells for 4 h. After incubation, the cells were washed three times with PBS and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 15 min. The cells were then washed again and stained with Prussian blue solution for 15 min. Finally, the cells were washed and examined under a microscope.

The Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF were labeled with rhodamine dye for fluorescent imaging. 4T1 cells were seeded in a 24-well plate (2 × 104 cells each well) and cultured for 24 h. A low concentration group (100 μg/mL) and a high concentration group (200 μg/mL) were established for the cells with and without the addition of free rutin. Subsequently, the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF-Rho were co-incubated with the cells for 2 h. Afterwards, the cells were washed three times with PBS and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 15 min. These cells were then washed again and stained with DAPI dye solution for 5 min under dark conditions. Following a final wash, the cells were examined under the fluorescence microscope.

The magnetic resonance imaging (MR) capability of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF was studied in vitro and in vivo. For in vitro imaging, 200 μL of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF solution with different concentrations (100, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, and 2000 μg/mL) were added into a 96-well plate, respectively. Subsequently, the samples were scanned using the SkyRAD 3.0 T superconducting MRI system (part of the Trio magnetic system, manufactured by Siemens, Germany). For in vivo imaging, Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF solution was injected into mice through the tail vein, and the measurement parameters were set to Tr = 3500 ms and Te = 89 ms, respectively. MRI scanner with a special coil specially designed for small animal imaging was then used to acquire T 1-weighted cross-sectional and sagittal images of the mice before and after the injection.

The cell scratch assay was employed to investigate the impact of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF on the migratory capacity of 4T1 cells. The experiment was divided into four groups: control group (RPMI 1640 medium), ZIF-8, PTX@ZIF, Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. 4T1 cells were seeded on a 6-well plate (1 × 106 cells each well) and cultured for 12 h. After these cells formed a monolayer covering over 80 % of the well bottom, a scratch was then made through the cell monolayer using the tip of a pipette, followed by careful washing with PBS. Subsequently, 0.5 mg/mL of ZIF-8, PTX@ZIF, and Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF solutions were added into these wells and incubated until time points (0, 6, 12, and 24 h), respectively. The migration and proliferation of the cells were observed using the inverted microscope.

The impact of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF on cell invasion ability in vitro was evaluated using a Transwell cell invasion assay. In this experiment, diluted Matrigel was pre-added into the upper chamber of the Transwell and placed in an incubator for 3 h to form a gel-like film. Then, excess liquid from the upper chamber was removed, and serum-free medium was added into each well for a pre-incubation period of 30 min. Next, 4T1 cells after incubation with different treatments (ZIF-8, PTX@ZIF, Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF) were collected and evenly distributed into the upper chamber of the Transwell plate (with 2 × 105 cells per well), while 500 μL of culture medium containing 10 % serum was added into the lower chamber. After 24 h of incubation, cells which have not invaded were removed with a cotton swab, and the remaining invading cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 30 min followed by staining with 0.1 % crystal violet for 15 min. Finally, the number of invading cells were counted using the inverted microscope and a brightfield optical system.

All of the 6-week-old female BALB/c mice were purchased from Changzhou Cavens Laboratory Animal Co., Ltd. in this study. Initially, the lower left breast of mice was subcutaneously injected with 100 μL of PBS containing 2 × 105 4T1 cells. When the tumor volume was approximately 100 mm3, these mice were divided into the five groups: Group I (PBS), Group II (ZIF-8 NPs), Group III (PTX@ZIF), Group IV (CCDs-ZIF) and Group V (Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF). The corresponding therapeutic drugs were injected through the tail vein. Group 1 was injected with 150 μL PBS, and the other four groups were injected with 150 μL of nanoparticles at a concentration of 200 μg/mL. Subsequently, the body weight and tumor volume of mice were measured every other day.

The Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF were successfully synthesized using the sequential self-assembed methods. The TEM image (Fig. 1A) showed that the ZIF-8 NPs displayed a uniform and monodisperse morphology with smooth surface. Meanwhile, the CCDs exhibited ultra-small particle shape (Fig. 1B), which were consistent with traditional CDs. Upon loading PTX and coating with CCDs and rutin, the surface of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF showed obvious granular and uneven bulges (Fig. 1C). DLS analysis indicated that the average diameters of ZIF-8 NPs, PTX@ZIF NPs, CCDs-PTX@ZIF NPs and Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF NPs were 114.3 nm, 118.9 nm, 176.7 nm, and 210.7 nm, respectively (Fig. 1D). These results suggested that PTX loading hardly affect the particle size, while CCDs loading and rutin coatimg significantly increased the particle size. Furthermore, the CCDs and rutin coatings also decreased the zeta potential of the PTX@ZIF NPs from 26.5 mV to 1.89 mV and − 7.66 mV (Fig. 1E), which were attributed to their negative charge characteristics. The X-ray diffraction spectrum of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF (Fig. 1F) showed special peaks at 7.4°, 12.7°, and 18.02°, which were consistent with characteristic peaks of ZIF-8 NPs.

Next, the surface composition and chemical groups of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF were investigated through the FTIR and XPS. Firstly, the FTIR spectrum showed O-H/N-H stretching vibrations at 3100–3600 cm−1, indicating the presence of carboxyl and amine groups in Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF (Fig. 1G). Additionally, the peaks at 2920, 1680, 1430, and 1290 cm−1 are attributed to CH, CO, CN, and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. These hydrophilic functional groups retained in the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF which contributed to their good water solubility. XPS spectrum showed that five main peaks at 1021.3, 717.3, 531.2, 399.8, and 1021.4 eV, which were consistent with to Zn, Fe, O, N and C element, respectively (Fig. 1H). The C1s peak was mainly from C1s (284.80 eV), indicating that C1s exists in various forms such as benzene rings, C − C, C − H, etc. The peaks for O element appeared at 531.25 eV and 530.59 eV. The peaks for N element were found at 399.72 eV and 399.14 eV. Two characteristic peaks of Zn ions located at 1044.53 eV and 1021.38 eV confirmed the presence of Zn (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. 1I, the Fe2p peak was deconvoluted into two peaks, primarily consisting of Fe2p (717.38 eV) corresponding to Fe3+, which suggested that Fe mainly exists in the form of trivalent iron in the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. These results demonstrated that the iron element was successfully introduced into Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. Elemental mapping further confirmed the presence of Zn and Fe in Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF (Figs. 1J, and S2). The loading capacity of PTX in Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF was determined to be 10.8 % via the HPLC method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase (POD)-like activity and killing effect of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the POD-like activity of CCDs was evaluated through the TMB and MV colorimetric method [57]. Previous studies showed that ·OH could oxidize colorless TMB to blue oxidized TMB, yet decompose purple MV into colorless products (Fig. 2A). It is worth noting that the only H2O2, whose colorlessness is due to the lack of strong ultraviolet-visible absorption bands, cannot catalyze the oxidation of TMB. However, Fe2+ ions react with H2O2 to produce the hydroxyl radical via Fenton reaction, resulting in the color change from the originally colorless to blue. Based on this, catalytic activity of CCDs for H2O2 production was estimated by the TMB assay(Fig. S3A). In the presence of H2O2, the CCDs obviously induced the fading of MV and decreased the intensity of the absorbance to 580 nm, which was similar to single Fe2+ ions treatment (Fig. S3B). Afterward, we attempted to use Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF instead of CCDs to verify the catalytic activity. The results showed Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF changed TMB color from colorless to blue and MV from purple to colorless (Fig. 2B and C). These findings clearly confirmed that CCDs possessed the POD-like ability to generate ·OH, and Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF also inherited this property.

We first evaluated the killing effect of CCDs on tumor cells using the CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. S4, when the CCDs concentration was 200 μg/mL, the cell survival rate was 76.06 %. Compared with normal tissues, the tumor microenvironment possessed very high concentration of H2O2 (up to 50 μM) due to the rapid proliferation of tumor cells [58]. Therefore, we speculated that CCDs with favorable POD-like activity could effectively kill tumor cells in the presence of hydrogen peroxide. As depicted in Fig. 2D, the CCDs were capable of inhibiting the viability of 4T1 cells on a concentration-dependent manner. When the concentration was high as 200 μg/mL, the cell viability sharply decreased to 14.7 %. Afterward, we further investigated the ability of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF which induced the production of ROS inside tumor cells. As shown in Fig. 2E, strong green fluorescence could be observed in the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF treatment, compared with week or no fluorescence in other treatments. The results showed that Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF can induce the generation of radicals and effectively catalyze H2O2 into highly oxidative ·OH in tumor, showing its potential value in tumor treatment.

To verify their biosafety in vivo, hemolysis test was carried out to evaluate the hemocompatibility of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF prior to in vivo administration. As we know, hemoglobin is released from red blood cells during hemolysis, resulting in a distinct absorption peak at 541 nm. Therefore, we can assess the hemocompatibility of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF by detecting this absorption peak. As shown in Fig. 3A, significant hemolysis could be observed clearly in the water-treated group (used as a positive control), which also showed a clear UV absorption peak at 541 nm and a brilliant red solution in line with it. In contrast, the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF-treated group and the PBS group (served as a negative control) did not exhibit a significant UV absorption peak at 541 nm, and the corresponding solutions of each group were almost transparent. Additional hemolysis rate calculations showed that the rate in various Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF groups stayed below 5 % even at concentrations of up to 600 μg/mL. These data preliminarily proved that the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF possessed favorable blood compatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tumor killing ability of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Compared to other drug carriers, ZIF-8 NPs are considered as an ideal nanoplatform due to their unique advantages, such as high drug loading, good biocompability and pH-responsive biodegradation property. Firstly, Fig. 3B showed that ZIF-8 NPs had little effect on MEF and 4T1 cell viability even at high concentration of 200 μg/mL (94 % and 92 %), indicating good cytocompatibility. Next, we assessed the killing effect of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF on tumor cells using the CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 3C, the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF treatment had stronger killing effect on 4T1 cells than PTX@ZIF treatment. At concentration of 200 μg/mL, the PTX@ZIF reduced cell survival rate to 21.5 %, while the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF significantly reduced cell survival rate to 14.3 %. In order to verify the effect of rutin modification on tumor cells, the killing effect of CCDs-PTX@ZIF and Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF on tumor cells was detected by CCK-8 method. As shown in the fig. S5, at concentration of 200 μg/mL, the CCDs-PTX@ZIF reduced cell survival rate to 19.2 %, with no significant difference compared with Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. The tumor killing effects of these two NPs were further confirmed by live/dead cell experiments (Fig. 3D). Compared to the control group and the ZIF-8 group, the PTX@ZIF and Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF groups exhibited almost all red fluorescence signals, indicating that these cells underwent an effective killing process. To thoroughly understand the antitumor mechanism of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF nanoparticles, we investigated their ability to induce apoptosis. Flow cytometry analysis in Fig. 4A showed that only 19.3 % of 4T1 cells in the PTX@ZIF treatment showed apoptotic translation, while 31.8 % of 4T1 cells in the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF treatment showed apoptotic translation. These experimental results demonstrated that Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF has significant tumor killing capability in vitro.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tumor cell targeting of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have shown that overexpressed GLUTs on the surface of tumor cell membranes may be selectively bound by glucose residues of rutin [35]. Therefore, we speculate that Ru-Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF can also specifically bind with tumor cells via rutin-mediated targeting. To investigate whether Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF nanoparticles exhibit targeting capabilities towards tumor cells, we incubated these nanoparticles with both 4T1 and MEF cells as a control. Following incubation, Prussian blue staining was employed to visualize the internalization of the nanoparticles within the cells. Our results revealed a notably higher uptake of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF in 4T1 cells compared to MEF cells when observed under identical conditions. This differential uptake strongly suggests that Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF possesses a selective targeting affinity for 4T1 tumor cells, indicating that Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF could target 4T1 cells (Fig. 4B). Subsequently, to confirm that Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF targeting of 4T1 cells is due to the specific binding of rutin to GLUTs, we designed a competitive experiment, including low (100 μg/mL) and high (200 μg/mL) concentration groups, with or without free rutin present. Before adding the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF, we pre-treated 4T1 cells with free rutin to block the GLUTs on the surface of tumor cells, thereby exploring the competitive interaction between tumor cells and the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. As shown in Fig. 4C, in the absence of free rutin, the tumor cells emitted strong red fluorescence, indicating the cellular uptake of rhodamine-labeled Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. However, the red fluorescence intensity decreased significantly when free rutin was added, which was attributed to blocked cellular uptake of rhodamine-labeled Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF due to competitive binding. Further, this trend was more obvious at higher concentrations (200 μg/mL). These findings showed that cellular uptake of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF was intimately linked to GLUTs-mediated endocytosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF on tumor cell migration and invasion in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we assessed the influence of the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF on the proliferation and migration of tumor cells via a wound scratch assay and transwell assay. As shown in Fig. 4D, 4T1 cells in the control group rapidly proliferated and moved over time, leading to the faster wound closure. In comparison, the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF treatment led to a delayed wound closure. Then, the wound healing rate was quantitatively analyzed by calculation of width of the wound field as shown in Fig. 4E. The results showed that the wound healing rate in the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF group was 17.7 %, which was lower than that in PTX@ZIF group (25 %).

Next, we evaluated the anti-invasive and anti-metastatic properties in vitro of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF through transwell invasion and migration assays on 4T1 cells. Compared with control and pure ZIF-8 treatment, both of PTX@ZIF and Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF significantly suppressed the invasion and metastasis ability of 4T1 cells (Fig. 5A and C). Moreover, Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF showed better inhibition of 4T1 cell motility than PTX@ZIF treatment group, which might be attributed to the production of OH- mediated by CCDs. As shown in Fig. 5A, a considerable number of migrating cells could be observed under the microscope in the control group, while the number of migrating cells was significantly reduced in the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF treated group. Calculations using Image J software revealed that the number of cell migration in Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF group was 94, which was lower than that in PTX@ZIF group (142)(Fig. 5B). We also obtained similar results in the cell invasion experiment (Fig. 5D). In the matrix gel that simulated the tumor invasion environment, the number of cell invasion in the PTX@ZIF group was 90, while that in the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF treatment group was significantly reduced to 54. This indicates that Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF significantly inhibited the invasive capacity of cancer cells, demonstrating its potential in suppressing 4T1 metastasis and invasion. For cancer cells, the strength of cloning ability in vitro is often related to the level of tumorigenesis in vivo. Therefore, the cell clonal formation assay was performed to evaluate the anticancer effect of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF in vitro. As shown in Fig. 5E, the clonoy forming ability of 4T1 cells was decreased under Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF treatment. Specifically, the number of living cell colonies in Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF treatment group (39) was significantly lower than that in PTX@ZIF group (55) (Fig. 5F). These results demonstrate that Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF possesses robust inhibiting activity, significantly reducing the migration, invasion, and proliferation of 4T1 tumor cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo and in vitro MR imaging of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the presence of the paramagnetic element iron, we hypothesized that Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF possess MR imaging capability. To test this hypothesis, we measured the T 1-weighted signal intensity of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF at various concentrations in vitro (Fig. 6A). The results showed that as the concentration of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF increased, the T1 image signal intensity gradually enhanced. By quantitative analysis, we found a direct proportional relationship between the T 1-weighted signal and Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF concentrations (Fig. 6B). Based on these findings, we designed experiments using tumor-bearing mice to explore the in vivo imaging function of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. Before and after the tail vein injection of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF, we recorded the T 1-weighted MR images of the tumors. As shown in Figs. 6C, a significant enhancement in signal intensity in the tumor area was observed under Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF treatment from the transverse position. As shown in Figs. 6D, the similar enhanced signal strength was also observed from the sagittal position. These results demonstrated that Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF had favorable MR imaging capability which can be used as an alternative contrast agent for MR imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antitumor effect of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the in vitro results, we further explored the antitumor effect of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF in a 4T1 tumor-bearing mouse model. When the tumor volume reached 100 mm3， the mice were randomly divided into five groups: control, ZIF-8, PTX@ZIF, CCDs-ZIF, and Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF. The images revealed that the obvious ulcer appeared in the first four groups (control, ZIF-8, PTX@ZIF, CCDs-ZIF), while the tumors in the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF group almost completely disappeared (Fig. 7A). As shown in Fig. 7B and C, the tumor growth was effectively inhibited for the first 5 days under PTX@ZIF and CCDs-ZIF treatment, but then grew rapidly. Surprisingly, the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF treatment group was able to suppress tumor growth throughout the observation period without recurrence. Additionally, the body weight curves of the mice (Fig. 7D) showed no significant fluctuations in all treatment groups, demonstrating that Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF might not cause significant side effects. As shown in Fig. 7E, the survival curves indicated that the mice in the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF group had an obviously longer life span compared with the other groups. The median survival time in the Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF group (60 days) was extended compared to that in the PBS group (29 days). Finally, the biosafety of Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF was assessed through histopathological sections. In the mice treated with Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF, no significant inflammation or lesions were observed in major organs (Liver, heart, spleen, lung, kidney), indicating that the nanoassembly had good biocompatibility in vivo (Fig. S6).

In summary, we designed a coordinated CDs-integrated ZIF-8 nanoassembly (Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF) by loading PTX and surface absorbing rutin for synergistic catalytic/chemo-therapy in breast cancer. The prepared Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF entered the tumor cells through the specific binding of rutin to GLUTs on the tumor cell membrane. The CDs-based single-atom nanozymes exhibit excellent POD activity, and the phenolic hydroxyl groups on CCDs can convert Fe3+ to Fe2+. These ferrous ions generated a large amount of hydroxyl radicals through the Fenton reaction, leading to tumor cell death. Due to the acid-responsive nature of the prepared Ru/CCDs-PTX@ZIF, paclitaxel can be released in the tumor environment, thereby inhibiting tumor cell division and inducing apoptosis. This self-assemble drug-loaded nanoplatform based on CDs provides a combined catalytic therapy strategy for breast cancer treatment.
